text:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have proven to be very effective in solving multi-faceted problems, such as natural language understanding, reasoning and more. However, they are currently not very reliable in carrying out exact scientific discovery due to the presence of hallucinations — generating valid statements that are however not true.

To address this challenge, we present a new evolutionary algorithm called FunSearch, or searching in the function space, which combines a pre-trained Language Model Learning (LLM) with a systematic evaluator. Current search algorithms are designed to find a particular solution. In contrast, our proposal searches for computer programs that indicate how to solve the problem at hand. Our proposal not only serves as a safeguard against LLM confabulations, but it also gives interpretable and scalable solutions.

We show that FunSearch can be used to solve some long-standing open problems. In extremal combinatorics, FunSearch was used to obtain new, larger constructions for the cap set problem, thus making the first LLM to contribute to a solution of an open mathematical problem that has been verified. More importantly, the new constructions obtained with the aid of FunSearch improve the cap set capacity lower bound for the first time in the last twenty years. Finally, we show that FunSearch can be also be used to obtain efficient algorithms for solving “practical” open problems. In particular, we have shown that FunSearch can be used to come up with new heuristics for the well-known online bin packing problem that improve upon the classical heuristic that is currently used in practice.

Our experiments suggest that search in the space of programs can yield remarkable breakthroughs that advance human understanding in heretofore intractable domains. The synergy of the LLMs’ creative, pattern recognition capabilities with the formal evaluator’s verification capabilities enables the system to discover program s that are not only of unprecedented proficiency but also comprehensible and easy to understand. This enables humans in the domain to read, understand, and use the output of the system, which in turn closes the loop and inspires further uses of the system.
explanation: Moderate likelihood the text is AI-generated. It is fluent and jargon-rich, but the misdefinition of LLM (Language Model Learning) and minor typos suggest AI authorship artifacts.
